params.text:
Confidentiality and the Caldicott Principles

Confidentiality means keeping personal information private and only using or sharing it appropriately. In health and social care, the Caldicott Principles guide how confidential information is used. The key ideas include:

  • Justify the purpose — be clear why you need the information.
  • Use confidential information only when it is really necessary.
  • Use the minimum necessary — don't share more than is needed.
  • Access should be strictly on a need-to-know basis.
  • Everyone must understand their responsibilities and comply with the law.
  • The duty to share information for care can be as important as the duty to protect confidentiality.

A Caldicott Guardian is a senior person in many services who champions the confidential and appropriate use of information.

params.alt: Paper records filed in a lockable cabinet drawer

params.taskDescription: Fill in the gaps to complete these confidentiality rules.
params.textField: Access to a service user's records should be strictly on a *need-to-know* basis. When information must be used, share the *minimum* amount necessary. A senior person known as the *Caldicott* Guardian champions confidentiality. Keeping personal details private is called *confidentiality*, and it should never be discussed in a public *corridor* where others might overhear.
